Open thread • Replies: 202 • Updated: 2026-07-08 21:59 UTC

OP

The more I look into investing and compare the historical returns of certain ETFs / individual stocks it seems like the whole philosophy of “VOO and Chill” is equal parts really smart and retarded
It’s like a no brainer for money but even now as long as the market is in a growth period shouldn’t you at least do QQQ?
It seems so simple that’d you miss a lot of upside but the returns are honestly not bad and extremely consistent
How accurate is this assessment? It’s both so simple and guaranteed that everyone should it but also too safe so you end up missing out on more upside
I’m talking 5-20 years investing here not trading

>I think a decade long CAGR comparison was a 5% difference but I’m not sure if CAGR is the best stat to go by?
It changes significantly depending on when you enter the market. Since 2000 there's only a 0.6% difference in favor of QQQ over SPY, the latter outperformed the former for most of that timeframe. If you bought sometime post-2009, however, you would have been better off holding QQQ by far. The moral of the story is keep an eye on things and only buy more volatile assets if it's a growth period. QQQ will continue to be great if you bought a decade ago until the music stops.

UNDER $10K NET WORTH THREAD #2

Open thread • Replies: 43 • Updated: 2026-07-08 21:36 UTC

OP

Brothers,

We spent 20 days and 203 posts together in the last thread and we have yet to formulate a method that will enable us to sit at the table in the six figure club with the rest of kinsmen. We knew this wouldn’t be easy so let's not get dispassionate.

For my next proposal I will say we focus on developing a skill that will increase our value. If we all level up on our own it will only take longer. If we all level up by 2-3 points a month then cumulatively we will all have gotten stronger as a mass society. Perhaps one amongst us can elevate themselves and come back to teach the others!

How long shall we be waiting then? How long will our own potential be mocked by others? To what limit will we continue to persist in the underclass?

Come, those who have not $10,000 to their name

Let us commence the general!

OP

Crypto/blockchain is finally, factually, concretely moving towards the integration we've been dreaming of (well not as cypher-punk as many of us have hoped and still hope for), yet the total crypto marketcap is not acting like it. Swift and the DTCC are getting on board, the BIS wrote a whole chapter about crypto in their annual economic report, we have the clarity act coming, etc. etc. Has AI just taken that large of a piece of the capital pie? I just don't get why more institutions are not piling into crypto right now. Are we waiting for pension funds, endowments, etc to be able to hold more than BTC/ETH? There's been a decent amount of price appreciation for select altcoins over the last year or so, but their mcaps are still far below previous tops. I guess those previous tops were often too feverish though...

item.title: I'm convinced most of the "it's over" crowd isn't representative of reality. — 8 replies — 32.0/hr
item.content:encoded:
I'm convinced most of the "it's over" crowd isn't representative of reality.

Open thread • Replies: 8 • Updated: 2026-07-08 22:01 UTC

OP

I'm convinced most of the "it's over" crowd isn't representative of reality. The guy who got a 4% raise, paid off his car, bought his first house, or maxed his Roth IRA doesn't make a thread because there's nothing interesting to say. The guy who blames AI, recruiters, HR, offshoring, boomers, landlords, and capitalism for every setback posts 20 times a day.

62461561
>>62461313
Sorry. No offense intended. But you have to pay attention for a little bit.
At the end of WWII, the USA had the world's most stable currency backed by most of the world's gold, most of the world's industrial capacity, and the world's most powerful military. The allied powers agreed at Breton Woods that the USA should issue the world's trade and reserve currency in the form of a dollar redeemable in gold by any signatory government. And so it went.

However, the US government, being greedy and unethical, began issuing dollars without regard to whether or not those dollars could be redeemed in gold.

The US had a free ride like nobody every in history, able to print as much fiat money as they wanted without any inflation. Eventually some cooler heads (Charles DeGaulle in particular) smelled a rat. France demanded redemption of all their dollars in gold. That's when Nixon reneged on Breton Woods. Dollars were no longer redeemable in gold, but the momentum of the dollar was so great that it continued to be the world's reserve and trade currency. Also some people got killed for trying to monkey with it.

None of this is a secret and you can bet China noticed what an amazingly profitable enterprise it is to be the issuer of the world's trade and reserve currency. They also have undoubtedly noticed that the dollar dominance was originally built on gold.

The CCP has done some incredibly stupid things (Cultural revolution, Great Leap Forward, One Child Policy, for example) but nobody needs to tell a Chinaman the value of gold. China sees cracks in dollar dominance and covets issuing a competing trade currency. It has said so publicly. But nobody trusts China. So what to do?

62461574
>>62461561
They stockpile enough gold to back the trade currency, create an international metals market outside the control of London and New York, and wait for the right moment of weakness to launch it. In short, China is going to remonetize gold. And the rest of the world, including the USA, will have follow to stay in the game. It would behoove you to have some in your portfolio.

62461583
>>62461476
People thinking there's a shortage of silver like it means anything. There's no shortage of silver as an industrial metal, you need so little silver and once it gets too pricey they'll switch to a different tech. Get gold unless you're gambling.

your best bet if you have a car is to get a job working overnight at McDonalds or another 24 hour fast food place

or just lie on your resume and get any menial wageslave job

if they ask about gaps in your resume you can say you were looking after a sick family member
make everything before the last 5 years or so a small business that you owned and operated yourself, for example lawnmowing

graduated high school
worked for yourself as a believable small business like a lawnmower
"my business was destroyed by covid"

worked as a dishwasher, or cleaning toilets, or some other thing for a business like a hotel that went out of business recently in your town or a nearby town. for 2 or so years until 6-12 months ago when that business closed
been searching for a job since then

- if they check references you're fucked but no one does for these dead end jobs like McDonalds maintainence or dishwasher. there are services you can hire which pretend to be references for you but it shouldn't be necessary
- if you don't have a car you're just fucked, you need it to have a leg up on other applicants for these jobs. I'm pretty sure I was hired by McDonalds the moment I said I had my own vehicle and could be available to start at 2am

>>62447595
I'm a Veteran. 7 years. Officer Corps. I did not get rich but I saw a few of my peers get rich and this advice only works if you're an Officer (single Enlisted are stuck in the barracks. Sucks to suck).

1) Do not get married
2) Do not spend money on women
3) Choose an MOS that deploys FREQUENTLY (you are guaranteed to deploy more frequently than Special Forces if you go Air Defense in the Army).
3) Get a VA loan to buy a Quadplex at your first duty station.
4) VA loan can only be taken out to buy your primary residence but if you PCS, you now need a new primary residence. You've built 2-3 years of equity in the first property, which you now rent out through a property management company.

Bonus: Set up your own property management company and start managing other peoples properties, too.

5) Repeat Steps 3 and 4 until you get out of the military.

6) Go to the doctor for literally anything and everything. When you separate you will have so many service connected injuries that youll get 100% VA Disability rating ($4k tax free each month) and with that rating, free medical coverage through the VA (a massive savings in medical costs)

You can also abuse the infinite certification and education programs they offer (and you should). They say not to leave the military with less education than you had going in. Take advantage of that. If you ever find yourself in a bullshit job where you're not in the field every 3 months, and you're able to take every 4 day weekend off without hassle, use that time to build a business of some sort.

Anyone who joins the military with a plan of action in place can really turn their life around. Maybe not into millionaire status, but into a comfortable, never NEED to work again type of life.

Why do people act like buying a house is a good investment

>Maintenance
>Repairs because something WILL go wrong
>Even if you're "beating inflation" with a mortgage you're paying much more than its actual cost
>Closing costs
>All just so you can sell it (likely at a loss when considering inflation) so you can buy a slightly less expensive house
It's not that buying a house is a bad purchase if you can afford it, but acting like it's somehow an "investment" is absolutely retarded, you'd probably make more back from buying Pokemon cards

62459719
>>62459227
>How much does that cost?
Typically 1-3% of the home value annually is the expectation on average but it seems more spaced out than that. I.e. roof goes bad 16k bill one year, next year maybe minimal expense basic maintenance items like filters, or an AC servicing for a few hundred.

>Optional if you own the home. Even then, I paid $1700 last year for insurance which is nothing. That’s easily 1 months rent by itself.
If you think home insurance is optional then lmao, I would never have a 300k+ possession that could be wiped out by some other idiots stupidity or natural disaster but you do you. Where I love its not too bad but certain states or areas have very high insurance costs (FL, CO, CA, etc.) Worth considering.

>Non issue
What if you get laid off? What if you get a job offer for 60% higher salary but it requires you to move 3 hours away?

>I can sell my house for more than I paid. You can’t sell the apartment that you dumped 5-6 figures into over the years. The choice is obvious
I can sell my shares of VOO too. It depends on where you live and if you inflation adjust property values. For example, where i live property has increased by 3.7% since 2023, that sounds pretty good until you realize that cumulative inflation was 9.84% over the same duration meaning you lost money relative to inflation in my region as a home owner. You also don't get to sell the interest the bank made off you during ownership, that piece of your mortgage payment (around 80%-85% for the first 5 years) is exactly like rent, poof. You also dont get back the real-estate transaction fees, realtor fee, and transfer taxes that is usually around 8-15k for a 300k house just gone. Homeowners also dump 5-6 figures into keeping their homes just in taxes usually. Let's be honest, owning a home is a nice thing but it costs you money and is not an asset, it is a necessity that you live somewhere but there are more efficient ways to use your capital if you want to be actually wealthy.

62460098
>>62459719
>$16k for a new roof
Who the fuck is paying $16k to have their roof reshingled? I paid $4500 years ago and I only did that because the old shingles were at least 20 years old and discolored. Other “maintenance and repair” expenses are minimal in the grand scheme of things.

>If you think home insurance is optional then lmao
It is… There is no law demanding a home owner, who owns a house that’s paid off (which mine is), to have insurance. The only reason I have it is because paying ~$1700ish a year is pocket change. I live in FL btw.

>what if…..
I’m not even going to bother. We can spend all day going back and forth playing this game.

When you buy a house, if you do, if you're going to rent it out, make sure you put a large enough down payment to make it cash flow $600+/mo.
Basically getting your money back little by little
and that 600 surplus will cover expenses as they come up, and someone else pays the equity for you, and you get the interest & depreciation deductions.
One big mistake with my rentals is putting the minimum down.
Expenses come out of my pocket mostly.
Remember taxes go up, insurance goes up, rezoned to a FEMA flood zone to lay for coastal homes, HOA goes up, everything goes up, so even if you increase rent, you're not taking it home, because you're paying those increased costs which go up every year.
You can make money in RE if you can fix the places yourself. If you're not handy? Think about something else.
